content: The STAR method structures behavioral answers: Situation (set the context in 1-2 sentences), Task (describe your specific responsibility), Action (detail the steps you took, using 'I' not 'we'), Result (quantify the outcome with numbers: saved $X, improved Y by Z%). Keep total response to 2-3 minutes.

Top behavioral question categories: Leadership (tell me about leading a project), Conflict Resolution (disagreement with a colleague), Failure (biggest mistake and what you learned), Initiative (going above and beyond), Adaptability (handling unexpected changes). Prepare 2-3 stories that cover multiple categories each.

The conflict resolution framework for interviews: 1) Acknowledge the disagreement professionally, 2) Seek to understand the other perspective, 3) Focus on shared goals not positions, 4) Propose a data-driven resolution, 5) Show the positive outcome. Never badmouth previous colleagues; emphasize collaboration and growth.

System design interview framework: 1) Requirements clarification (functional + non-functional, 5 min), 2) Back-of-envelope estimation (QPS, storage, bandwidth), 3) High-level design (API + data model + architecture diagram, 10 min), 4) Detailed design (dive deep into 2-3 components, 15 min), 5) Bottlenecks and trade-offs (scaling, failure modes, 5 min).

System design estimation shortcuts: 1 day = 86,400 seconds (round to 100K). 1 million requests/day = 12 requests/second. 1 GB = 1 billion bytes. Typical web page is 2-3 MB. A tweet-like text record is ~250 bytes. 1 image thumbnail ~50 KB, full image ~500 KB. 1 minute of video ~150 MB at 1080p.

Coding interview approach: 1) Clarify inputs, outputs, edge cases, and constraints (2-3 min), 2) Discuss brute force solution and its complexity, 3) Optimize with better data structures or algorithms, 4) Code the solution cleanly with meaningful variable names, 5) Trace through examples and test edge cases. Talk through your thinking aloud.

Common coding interview patterns: Two Pointers (sorted arrays), Sliding Window (subarrays), Fast/Slow Pointers (linked list cycles), Merge Intervals, BFS/DFS (trees, graphs), Binary Search (sorted data), Dynamic Programming (optimization with overlapping subproblems), Topological Sort (dependencies), Trie (string search), Union-Find (connected components).

Salary negotiation principles: Research market range on Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, and Blind (accuracy varies by source). Negotiate total compensation (base + equity + bonus + benefits), not just salary. Delay stating a number first; say 'I'd like to understand the full package before discussing numbers.' Counter-offer 10-20% above their initial.

When countering a salary offer, use competing offers as leverage without being adversarial. Frame it as: 'I'm very excited about this role. I have another offer at $X and would love to make this work. Is there flexibility in the compensation to close the gap?' Companies typically have 10-15% negotiation room on base and can adjust equity/signing bonus.

Company research checklist: Read the 10-K annual filing (revenue, growth rate, risks), study the product (use it for 1-2 hours), check Glassdoor reviews (filter for your role), read recent news and earnings calls, understand the competitive landscape, and identify 2-3 specific improvements you'd suggest. Reference these in your 'Why this company?' answer.

10-K filing key sections for interview prep: Item 1 (Business description and strategy), Item 1A (Risk factors — shows company challenges), Item 7 (Management discussion — revenue trends, growth drivers), and the financial statements (revenue growth %, margins, cash flow). SEC EDGAR provides free access to all public company filings.

Send a personalized thank-you email within 24 hours of each interview. Reference a specific topic discussed ('I enjoyed our conversation about the migration to microservices'). Reiterate your enthusiasm and 1-2 key qualifications. Keep it to 3-4 sentences. For panel interviews, send individual notes to each interviewer with unique references.

Panel interview strategy: Make eye contact with the person asking the question but periodically address others. Note each interviewer's name and role at the start. Tailor answers to their perspective (engineering lead gets technical depth, product manager gets impact/metrics, VP gets strategic alignment). Engage quieter panelists with a brief glance or inclusive language.

Remote/video interview setup: Test camera, microphone, and internet speed (minimum 10 Mbps upload) 1 hour before. Use a ring light or face a window for even lighting. Position camera at eye level. Use a neutral background or professional virtual background. Close all other apps to prevent notifications. Have a backup plan (phone hotspot, dial-in number).

Minimize remote interview distractions: Use headphones with a microphone to reduce echo and background noise. Put phone on Do Not Disturb. Close email and chat applications. Keep water and notes nearby. If connection drops, rejoin within 30 seconds and briefly apologize without over-explaining. Practice screen-sharing your IDE beforehand.

Portfolio presentation for technical roles: Select 3-5 projects that demonstrate range (frontend, backend, full-stack, data). For each project: state the problem, your approach, and quantified impact (reduced load time by 40%, served 10K daily users, saved $50K/year). Have a live demo or screenshots ready. Prepare for deep-dive questions on architecture decisions.

Quantifying project impact for interviews: Use the formula 'Improved X by Y% resulting in Z.' Examples: 'Reduced API latency from 800ms to 200ms (75% improvement), improving user retention by 12%.' 'Automated manual process saving 20 hours/week across the team.' 'Built feature adopted by 85% of users within 30 days of launch.'

The 'Tell me about yourself' answer should be 60-90 seconds using the Present-Past-Future structure: Present (current role and key achievement), Past (relevant experience and skills that led here), Future (why this role excites you and how you add value). Avoid personal details unless they directly relate to the role.

Questions to ask the interviewer (prepare 5-8, ask 2-3 per round): 'What does success look like in the first 90 days?', 'What's the biggest technical challenge the team faces?', 'How does the team handle code reviews and technical decisions?', 'What's the path for growth in this role?', 'Why did you choose to join/stay at this company?'

Red flags to watch for during interviews: No clear job description, interviewer is unprepared or distracted, unrealistic expectations (10x engineer), high turnover signals ('we're rebuilding the team'), unwillingness to discuss compensation range, pressure to decide immediately, and negative comments about current employees.

Whiteboard coding tips: Write clearly and large enough for everyone to read. Start with function signature and return type. Leave space between lines for insertions. Talk through your approach before writing code. Use abbreviations only if you define them. Step through your code with a simple example after writing. Mark where you'd add error handling.

Take-home assignment best practices: Timebox to the recommended duration (typically 3-5 hours). Include a README with setup instructions, design decisions, and trade-offs. Write tests for core logic (aim for 70%+ coverage on critical paths). Use clean git history with meaningful commits. Note what you'd improve given more time.

Handling the 'weakness' question: Choose a genuine but non-critical weakness. Use past tense to show improvement: 'I used to struggle with delegating tasks. I've since learned to break projects into clear sub-tasks and trust team members, which improved our sprint velocity by 25%.' Avoid cliches like 'I'm a perfectionist.'

Interview timeline management: Apply to 10-15 positions in the same 1-2 week window to align interview timelines. Most processes take 2-4 weeks from first screen to offer. When you receive an offer, ask for 1 week to decide (standard). Use the deadline to accelerate other processes: 'I have an offer with a deadline on Friday.'

Mock interview preparation: Practice with a partner using real questions from Glassdoor and LeetCode. Record yourself answering behavioral questions and review for filler words (um, like), pace (target 130-150 words/minute), and clarity. Do at least 3 full mock system design interviews with timing. Use Pramp or Interviewing.io for peer practice.
